questions.question: Explain the difference between system calls and library functions with examples.
keyPoints: System calls transition from user mode to kernel mode while library functions execute entirely in user mode
System calls provide OS services directly (e.g., read, write, fork)
Library functions are utility functions (e.g., printf, malloc) that may internally use system calls
System calls have higher overhead due to mode switch

questions.question: Describe the six categories of system calls with one example for each.
keyPoints: Process Control: fork(), exec(), wait() - for creating and managing processes
File Management: open(), read(), write(), close() - for file operations
Device Management: ioctl(), read(), write() - for device I/O
Information Maintenance: getpid(), uname(), date() - for system/process info
Communication: pipe(), socket(), msgget() - for IPC
Protection: chmod(), chown(), umask() - for access control

questions.question: What is a file descriptor? Explain with reference to standard file descriptors.
keyPoints: File descriptor is a non-negative integer identifying an open file
Standard file descriptors: 0 (stdin), 1 (stdout), 2 (stderr)
Assigned automatically at process creation
Used in read(), write(), close() system calls

questions.question: Explain the fork() system call with its return values.
keyPoints: fork() creates a new process (child) by duplicating the parent
Returns child's PID (positive) to parent process
Returns 0 to the newly created child process
Returns -1 to parent if creation fails (sets errno)
Child process gets copy of parent's address space

questions.question: What is a zombie process? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Zombie is a terminated process whose entry remains in process table
Occurs when parent doesn't call wait() to retrieve child's exit status
Prevents by: parent calling wait()/waitpid(), ignoring SIGCHLD signal, using double fork technique
If not cleaned, zombie processes can exhaust PID table

questions.question: Describe the sequence of operations when a process executes the read() system call.
keyPoints: Process invokes read() from user mode
System call interface saves process state and switches to kernel mode
Kernel validates file descriptor and permissions
Kernel copies data from disk buffer to user buffer
Returns to user mode with bytes read or error code

questions.question: Explain the purpose of pipe() system call in inter-process communication.
keyPoints: pipe() creates a unidirectional communication channel
Returns two file descriptors: read-end and write-end
Data written to write-end can be read from read-end
Used for parent-child communication or sibling processes
Forms basis for shell pipelines (cmd1 | cmd2)

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the execution of fork() system call and differentiate between parent and child process execution.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: Original process → fork() → Two processes (parent and child)
Parent receives child's PID as return value
Child receives 0 as return value
Both processes continue execution from after fork()
Child gets copy of parent's address space (data, heap, stack)
Child has new PID and PPID pointing to parent
file descriptors are duplicated (reference same open file table entry)
After fork(), typically child does exec() to run new program

questions.question: Explain the various file management system calls in UNIX with their syntax and return values.
keyPoints: open(path, flags, mode): Returns file descriptor or -1 on error - opens/creates file
read(fd, buffer, n): Returns bytes read or -1 - reads data from file
write(fd, buffer, n): Returns bytes written or -1 - writes data to file
lseek(fd, offset, whence): Returns new position or -1 - repositions file pointer
close(fd): Returns 0 or -1 - closes open file descriptor
creat(path, mode): Equivalent to open with O_CREAT|O_WRONLY|O_TRUNC
dup(fd): Duplicates file descriptor

questions.question: Discuss the protection mechanism in UNIX operating system with reference to system calls.
keyPoints: User ID (UID) and Group ID (GID) identify users and groups
File permissions: read (r), write (w), execute (x) for owner, group, others
chmod() changes file permissions with numeric (755) or symbolic modes
chown() changes file ownership
umask() sets default creation mask for new files
System calls check permissions before allowing operations
Process permissions verified against file permissions
Superuser (UID=0) bypasses most permission checks

questions.question: Explain the process creation and termination system calls in UNIX with their interrelationship.
keyPoints: fork(): Creates new process by duplicating parent
exec(): Replaces process image with new program after fork
wait(): Parent synchronizes with child termination
exit(): Process terminates and returns status to parent
Zombie state: Terminated process waiting for parent to call wait()
Orphan process: Child whose parent terminated (adopted by init)
Signal handling: Processes notified of events (SIGCHLD, SIGTERM, SIGKILL)
getpid(), getppid(): Get current and parent process IDs
